## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
Global geopolitical tensions remain high, particularly in the Middle East where renewed US-Iran strikes have intensified, affecting tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and causing oil prices to strengthen. Despite ongoing diplomatic efforts, including an Iranian delegation traveling to Oman for negotiations, a dramatic exchange of fire has derailed a previous truce. India and New Zealand have elevated their bilateral ties to a strategic partnership, focusing on defense, security, and trade, during Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi's visit to Auckland. South Africa has requested an exemption from proposed US tariffs linked to an investigation into forced labor in imports, asserting its strong laws against the practice.

---

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
The US government's 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, aimed at expanding affordable housing and reducing costs, has become law without the President's signature. Separately, the US and Canada have agreed to open the Gordie Howe International Bridge on July 27, a project expected to bolster North American supply chains and manufacturing.

### Europe
Europe is considering proposals to implement navigational fees in the Strait of Hormuz, with plans specifying that these tolls must not be compulsory. The EU has also condemned "genocide denial" and the glorification of convicted war criminals ahead of the 31st anniversary of the Srebrenica massacre. France has activated its Extreme Heat ORSEC emergency plan for the first time, opening cooling centers in all departments under a red heat wave alert, while Belgium recorded 1,747 excess deaths during a June heat wave.

### Asia-Pacific
Typhoon Bavi has impacted Japan's southern islands and prompted the evacuation of over 600,000 people in China as it moves towards the mainland. The Philippines has also experienced significant effects from Typhoon Bavi, with a rising death toll and numerous international flight cancellations. Governments across Asia and the Pacific have adopted a new regional agenda to address climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution through more coordinated actions. India and New Zealand have established a strategic partnership during PM Modi's visit to Auckland, expanding cooperation across various sectors.

### Africa
Mali and Algeria have reopened their airspace and reinstated ambassadors, ending a year-long diplomatic dispute that began after Algeria shot down a Malian armed drone. The African Union Commission Chairperson Mahmoud Ali Youssouf urged African countries to move beyond anti-corruption laws and foster a culture of integrity and accountability. In Congo, a U.S. citizen working for a humanitarian organization has tested positive for the Ebola virus, as the country continues to battle a growing outbreak caused by the rare Bundibugyo virus which lacks an approved vaccine or treatment. Kenya has extradited an Afghan national to the US on narcotics trafficking and firearms charges, highlighting cooperation in combating transnational organized crime.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region
Tensions in the Middle East have escalated with renewed US-Iran strikes impacting the Strait of Hormuz, leading to concerns over oil supply disruptions. Iran has denied requesting talks with the US and warned of "reciprocal action" against any breach by Washington, while President Trump has issued threats of "1000 Missiles Locked and Loaded" should Iran attempt an assassination. Mediators are working to de-escalate the situation and urge a return to diplomacy and dialogue. Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman engaged in a direct call with President Trump to discuss bilateral cooperation and maritime security, signaling Riyadh's efforts to re-insert itself into the ongoing peace process negotiations in Islamabad.

## SECTION 4 — Technology and the Future
Japan's space agency, JAXA, successfully conducted the first test flight for its experimental reusable rocket, RV-X, demonstrating technology crucial for reducing launch costs and competing in the global space market dominated by SpaceX. This follows China's reported achievement of its first successful controlled recovery of a carrier rocket's first stage. SpaceX also launched 29 Starlink broadband satellites into low Earth orbit on a Falcon 9 rocket, marking its 35th flight for that booster, just one shy of the company's reuse record. NASA is seeking volunteers for year-long simulated Moon and Mars deep space missions, aimed at preparing for future human spaceflight endeavors.

## SECTION 5 — Tech Industry & Global Business Shifts
The cybersecurity landscape has seen significant incidents in 2026, including data breaches affecting Instructure, Conduent, and Xsolis, highlighting vulnerabilities in third-party technology providers and legacy systems. A critical zero-day vulnerability in Oracle PeopleSoft was exploited, leading to a major regulatory data breach for the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC). The theft of proprietary AI models from industry leaders has also signaled a new era of industrial espionage, with Novo Nordisk confirming unauthorized access to internal IT systems where some non-public data was copied. Agentic ransomware, driven by large language models, has emerged with the discovery of "JadePuffer" by the Sysdig Threat Research Team.

The medical field is facing challenges with the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak in Congo, caused by the rare Bundibugyo virus, for which no approved vaccine or treatment currently exists. ## SECTION 7 — Global Health & Biosecurity Watch
A U.S. citizen in Congo, working for a humanitarian organization, has tested positive for the Ebola virus amidst the country's ongoing and rapidly expanding outbreak. The Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention reported 1,830 confirmed cases and 648 deaths in Congo, with cases also confirmed in neighboring Uganda. The outbreak is attributed to the Bundibugyo virus, for which there are currently no approved vaccines or treatments. The World Health Organization (WHO) and public health authorities are working to prevent further transmission and identify close contacts.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)
At least 15 Indian tourists tragically died after a boat capsized near Phu Quoc Island in Vietnam, with Prime Minister Narendra Modi expressing grief and assuring assistance. PM Modi is currently in Auckland, New Zealand, marking the first visit by an Indian PM in 40 years, with objectives including doubling bilateral trade by 2030 and exploring the linkage of India's UPI with New Zealand's payment system. The Indian Navy officially commissioned the sixth advanced P17A-class stealth frigate 'INS Mahendragiri' in Visakhapatnam. Violence erupted in Madhya Pradesh's Datia district following clashes over a denied BJP ticket, and landslides and floods are impacting Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, and Jammu & Kashmir due to intensified monsoon activity. The Supreme Court is slated to hear pleas on July 13 regarding an independent investigation into the finances of the Ram Temple Trust. India's first hydrogen train is expected to be flagged off next week from Haryana, and Mumbai observed the 20th anniversary of the 7/11 serial train blasts. The Enforcement Directorate (ED) attached assets valued at ₹1,021 crore in the ongoing Reliance Anil Ambani Group case.

## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates
Telangana is considering deploying Demographically Similar Entries (DSE) software for the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) 2026 to identify and remove duplicate voter enrolments. Chief Minister A Revanth Reddy expressed profound shock over the Vietnam boat accident involving tourists from Telugu states and directed officials to provide assistance; a control room has been established. Panchayat Raj, Rural Development, and Women & Child Welfare Minister Danasari Anasuya Seethakka was deeply shocked by the brutal murder of six individuals in Daivalaguda, Ranga Reddy district. Union Coal and Mines Minister G Kishan Reddy highlighted Hyderabad's emergence as a key technology and infrastructure hub in India. All schools in Telangana are open today, July 11, to compensate for academic days lost earlier in the year, overriding the usual Second Saturday holiday. Telangana Rakshana Sena chief Kalvakuntla Kavitha alleged that the Congress government and Singareni management are attempting to suppress workers' voices. KTR (Kalvakuntla Taraka Rama Rao) challenged Chief Minister Revanth Reddy to face elections and accused the Congress of leading Telangana towards drought. Bala Manvitha Reddy advanced to the semifinals of the Telangana badminton tournament. KV Golconda secured the girls' title at the Eddie Aibara junior cricket tournament.

A 46-year-old man in Kadapa district died of COVID-19, marking the state's first COVID-related death in four years, prompting contact tracing. Andhra Pradesh Minister Nara Lokesh reviewed the Vietnam boat accident, directing support for affected tourists, with three Andhra natives identified among the 15 Indian fatalities. Andhra Pradesh is focusing on green growth and energy-driven investments as part of its "Swarna Andhra Vision 2047," aiming for 100% renewable energy integration and 5 lakh new green jobs. Union Defence Minister Rajnath Singh commissioned the 'INS Mahendragiri' stealth frigate in Visakhapatnam, emphasizing Andhra Pradesh's growing role as a defense and aerospace manufacturing hub. The state's Green Hydrogen Policy (2024–29) is designed to attract over ₹30,000 crore in hydrogen investments over the next five years. The YSRCP party condemned the reported destruction of standing crops in the capital region.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** Tensions between the United States and Iran escalated significantly this week, marked by renewed airstrikes and reciprocal threats, particularly concerning the Strait of Hormuz. A previous truce, established under a mid-June memorandum of understanding, appears to have crumbled following Iranian strikes on commercial vessels and subsequent US retaliatory attacks. President Trump has issued strong warnings to Iran, while Iran has denied seeking talks and threatened "reciprocal action" if provoked.
**Root Causes:** The underlying causes stem from long-standing geopolitical rivalry, differing interpretations of the mid-June truce agreement, and the strategic importance of the Strait of Hormuz for global oil transit. Internal factions within Iran, described as "errant hardliners," are also cited as potentially undermining diplomatic efforts by carrying out attacks on commercial ships.
**Historical Background:** The current flare-up follows a period of easing tensions after approaching pre-conflict levels in late June. The Strait of Hormuz has historically been a flashpoint, facilitating a significant portion of global crude oil and petroleum product movement. Previous closures triggered substantial oil price surges, with Brent crude peaking at approximately $126 per barrel during the height of past disruptions.
**Key Actors:** Key actors include the United States (President Trump, US military), Iran (Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, Supreme National Security Council secretary Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr, alleged "hardliners"), and mediating countries such as Oman, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Pakistan, Egypt, and Qatar. Israel also remains a player, with its Defence Minister stating readiness to resume military campaigns against Iran.
**Strategic Importance:** The escalation directly impacts global energy security due to the critical role of the Strait of Hormuz in oil and LNG supply chains. The disruption of tanker traffic has already caused Brent crude prices to rise. The situation also tests international diplomatic efforts and could lead to a broader regional conflict, affecting trade routes, energy markets, and regional stability.
**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Long-term consequences could include sustained higher energy costs, reconfigured shipping routes to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, increased investment in alternative energy sources, and a further polarization of regional alliances. A continued breakdown of diplomatic channels risks entrenching a cycle of military confrontation, impacting global economic stability and humanitarian conditions in the region.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   In 1798, the U.S. Marine Corps was formally re-established by a congressional act that also created the U.S. Marine Band.
*   In 1804, Vice President Aaron Burr mortally wounded former Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton during a pistol duel in Weehawken, New Jersey.
*   In 1859, Big Ben, the great bell inside the famous London clock tower, chimed for the first time.
*   In 1914, Babe Ruth made his Major League Baseball debut, pitching for the Boston Red Sox.
*   In 1960, Harper Lee's classic novel "To Kill a Mockingbird" was first published.
*   In 1979, the abandoned U.S. space station Skylab re-entered Earth's atmosphere, showering debris over the Indian Ocean and Australia.
*   In 1991, Nigeria Airways Flight 2120 crashed in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, killing all 261 passengers and crew.
*   In 1995, diplomatic relations between the United States and Vietnam were established.
*   Also in 1995, the U.N.-designated "safe haven" of Srebrenica fell to Bosnian Serb forces, leading to the massacre of over 8,000 Muslim men and boys.
*   In 2010, the FIFA World Cup Final was held in Johannesburg, South Africa, with Spain defeating the Netherlands.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   In 1997, ten persons were killed as police opened fire on a Dalit mob in Mumbai protesting the desecration of an Ambedkar bust.
*   In 2000, noted Punjabi writer Amrita Pritam won the Shatabdi Samman award.
*   In 2006, Mumbai experienced a series of serial train blasts, killing over 200 people.
*   In 1957, Aga Khan III, the 48th Imam of the Nizari Ismaili sect, who played an important role in Indian constitutional reforms, passed away.
*   In 2003, Bhishma Sahni, a famous Indian writer and leading author in Hindi literature, passed away.
*   In 1882, Baba Kanshi Ram, a great freedom fighter and social reformer, was born at Dada Siba village, Kangra.

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development
An important but underreported development is the increasing weaponization of AI in cyber warfare, as evidenced by the emergence of "agentic ransomware" like JadePuffer, which operates end-to-end driven by large language models. This signifies a critical shift in the cyber threat landscape, where attacks can become more autonomous, sophisticated, and rapidly adaptive, potentially outpacing traditional human-led defenses. The collaboration between cybercriminal groups, such as Vect and TeamPCP, to combine specialized capabilities like credential theft and ransomware-as-a-service, further highlights the evolving, business-like structure of organized cybercrime, posing a systemic risk to global digital infrastructure and intellectual property.
